name: Purpose
subtitle: Level 1 · Overwhelm · Aligned Vision
whatThisIs:
Purpose is your filter. Without it, every opportunity looks the same, every decision feels hard, and every day feels heavy. You say yes to things that drain you and no to things that matter because you have no clear line to judge them against.

This playbook is not about finding your life's calling or writing a mission statement that sounds good on a website. It is about defining what you are actually willing to commit to, the problem you want to solve, the change you want to create, and the people you want to serve. Once that is clear, choices become simple.

Purpose at Level 1 is practical, not philosophical. It answers: what am I building this business to achieve, and is what I am doing today moving me towards that or away from it?

broken.label: When this is broken
broken.content: Everything feels equally important and equally pointless. You take on clients or projects you do not enjoy because they pay the bills, but you resent the work. You change direction constantly based on the latest opportunity or advice. You cannot explain why someone should choose your business over a competitor beyond price or convenience. Every day feels like pushing a boulder uphill.

ok.label: When this is OK
ok.content: You have a rough sense of what you want to achieve but it changes based on your mood or the latest challenge. Sometimes you feel aligned with your work, other times it feels like a grind. You can explain what you do but struggle to explain why it matters beyond making money. You make decisions based on gut feel rather than clear criteria.

working.label: When this is working
working.content: You can clearly explain the change you exist to create and why it matters to you personally. You know exactly who you serve and who you do not serve, and you are comfortable saying no to good money from the wrong people. Your daily work feels connected to something bigger. When opportunities arise, you can quickly decide if they align with your purpose or distract from it.

thingsToThinkAbout: What transformation are you truly committed to delivering? Not what you think sounds impressive, but what you actually want to see happen in the world. If you strip away the revenue, the recognition, and the external validation, what change would you still want to create?

Who are you unwilling to build this business around? Purpose becomes real when you define who you will not serve. Which clients, customers, or market segments would you turn down even if they offered good money? This boundary tells you more about your purpose than any mission statement.

If income were guaranteed, would this still matter to you? Imagine you had enough money to never work again. Would you still care about the problem your business solves? If the answer is no, you might be solving the wrong problem, or you might need to reframe why it matters to you.

Is this a problem you want to solve for the next decade? Purpose is not a sprint, it is the thing you are willing to commit to long term. Can you see yourself still caring about this problem in ten years, or are you just solving it because you happen to be good at it right now?

Where is your daily behaviour misaligned with your stated purpose? Look at how you actually spend your time, which clients you take on, and which projects get your best energy. If there is a gap between what you say matters and how you actually behave, either your purpose needs refining or your choices need changing.
actionsIntro: When you open this playbook in the Profit System, you will find:
quickWins: This week: Write down the last three business decisions you made and ask: "Did I make this choice because it aligned with what I want to achieve, or because I felt I had to?"
This week: List your current clients or customers. Circle the ones you genuinely enjoy working with. Ask yourself what they have in common, that is a clue to your purpose.
This week: Complete this sentence: "If I could fix one problem in my industry forever, it would be..." Your first answer is usually the most honest.

detailSections.title: What to Do
detailSections.content: Define the specific change you exist to create, in clear, practical language. Not a fluffy mission statement for your website — a decision-making filter you will use every day to choose what to pursue and what to ignore.

detailSections.title: Why It Matters
detailSections.content:
Without a clear purpose, every opportunity looks the same. You say yes to work that drains you, chase trends that distract you, and build a business that looks successful from the outside but feels hollow from the inside. Mike Michalowicz calls this "Lifestyle Congruence" — your business should serve the life you actually want, not the other way around. Simon Sinek's insight is simpler: people do not buy what you do, they buy why you do it. If you cannot articulate your "why," neither can your customers — and your marketing, sales, and hiring all suffer.

A good purpose statement is not inspirational wallpaper. It is a filter that makes hard decisions easy.

detailSections.title: How to Use This
detailSections.content:
Work through the steps in order. The first draft will not be perfect — that is fine. A rough purpose statement you actually use beats a polished one that sits in a drawer.

---

detailSections.title: Step 1: Find the Raw Material
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Complete this sentence: "The problem I want to solve is..."** — Not what you sell. The underlying problem that drew you to this work in the first place. Be specific — "help businesses grow" is too vague. "Help overwhelmed trade business owners stop doing everything themselves" is a purpose.
- [ ] **Complete this sentence: "The people I care most about helping are..."** — Not your broadest possible market. The specific group whose problem you understand deeply because you have lived it, studied it, or seen it up close for years.
- [ ] **Complete this sentence: "If money were not a factor, I would still care about this because..."** — This separates real purpose from convenient positioning. If you would walk away the moment you got rich, your purpose needs more honesty.
- [ ] **Complete this sentence: "I am uniquely positioned to do this because..."** — What combination of experience, skill, and perspective do you bring that most others do not? This is not about being the best — it is about being distinctive.

detailSections.title: Step 2: Draft Your Purpose Statement
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Combine your answers into one statement: "I exist to [change/transformation] for [specific people] because [why it matters to you]"** — Keep it under 30 words. If you cannot say it in one breath, it is too complicated.
- [ ] ⚡ Quick Win · **Read it out loud to someone who does not know your business** — If they can immediately understand what you do and why, it is working. If they look confused, simplify.
- [ ] **Test it against your last 5 business decisions** — Would this statement have helped you decide faster? If it does not change how you make decisions, it is not specific enough.

detailSections.title: Step 3: Pressure Test
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Does it exclude people?** — A purpose that applies to everyone is a purpose that guides nothing. You should be able to name specific types of clients or work that fall outside your purpose.
- [ ] **Does it energise you?** — Read it again tomorrow morning. Does it make you want to get to work, or does it feel like corporate filler? If the latter, go back to Step 1 and be more honest.
- [ ] **Could a competitor copy it word for word?** — If yes, it is too generic. Your purpose should reflect something specific about your experience, perspective, or approach.

---

detailSections.title: Expected Outcome
detailSections.content:
- A clear, specific purpose statement under 30 words
- A decision-making filter you can apply to every opportunity, client, and project
- Clarity on who you serve and who you do not — which makes marketing, hiring, and strategy dramatically easier
- A foundation for your Core Offer, Ideal Customer, and Brand work

detailSections.title: Coach Notes
detailSections.content:
Most clients will resist being specific because specificity feels like limiting their options. Reframe it: specificity is not about doing less — it is about doing the right things with full commitment instead of everything at half effort.

If a client says "I just want to help people and make money," that is honest but not useful. Dig deeper: which people? Help them with what? Why you and not someone else? The coaching value is in the questions that draw out what they already know but have not articulated.

The purpose statement will evolve. Do not let perfection stall progress — a first draft they can test is more valuable than a perfect statement they never write.

detailSections.title: What to Do
detailSections.content: Create two clear lists: the clients and work you actively want to pursue (Serve), and the clients and work you will decline even when they offer money (Swerve). This turns your purpose statement from an idea into a boundary you can enforce.

detailSections.title: Why It Matters
detailSections.content: Purpose without boundaries is just a nice thought. Michalowicz's Pumpkin Plan makes this brutally clear: the businesses that grow fastest are the ones that identify their best clients, cut the rest, and pour all their energy into the winners. Most business owners do the opposite — they take on anyone who can pay, dilute their focus, and end up resenting the work. The Serve & Swerve analysis forces the decision: who deserves your best effort, and who is costing you more than they pay?

detailSections.title: How to Use This
detailSections.content:
Work through both sides honestly. The Swerve list is usually harder — it means saying no to money. That is the point.

---

detailSections.title: The Serve List — Who You Want
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **List your 5 best clients or customers from the past 2 years** — Best means: profitable, enjoyable, referred others, valued your work, and got great results. Not just "paid on time."
- [ ] **What do they have in common?** — Look for patterns: industry, business size, personality type, problem they came to you with, stage of business. The pattern is your ideal client profile.
- [ ] **What made working with them great?** — Was it their attitude? Their willingness to implement? The type of problem? Their respect for your process? Name the specific qualities.
- [ ] **Write your Serve criteria** — "I actively want to work with people who are [description], facing [problem], and who [quality/behaviour]." This becomes your intake filter.

detailSections.title: The Swerve List — Who You Avoid
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **List your 5 worst client experiences** — The ones that drained your energy, caused problems, or were unprofitable. Be specific about what made them bad.
- [ ] **What do they have in common?** — Again, look for patterns. Industry? Attitude? Budget? Expectations? The pattern tells you who to avoid.
- [ ] ⚡ Quick Win · **Write your red flags** — 3-5 warning signs you can spot in a sales conversation that tell you this person will be a bad fit. These save you months of frustration per bad client avoided.
- [ ] **Write your Swerve criteria** — "I will decline work from people who [description]." Make it specific enough to actually use.

detailSections.title: The Hard Decisions
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Review your current client list against both criteria** — Who on your current roster would not pass the Serve filter today? You do not need to fire them all tomorrow, but you need to see the reality.
- [ ] **Identify clients you are keeping out of guilt, fear, or habit** — These are the ones draining your energy and displacing better clients. Plan a graceful exit for at least one.
- [ ] **Calculate the cost of your worst client** — Not just revenue, but time, energy, stress, and opportunity cost. What could you do with that time if they were gone?

---

detailSections.title: Expected Outcome
detailSections.content:
- A clear definition of who you actively pursue and who you decline
- Red flag criteria you can use in sales conversations to filter fast
- The courage to say no to bad-fit clients — backed by data, not just gut feel
- A current client roster review that shows where you are aligned and where you are drifting
- Over time, a client base that energises you instead of draining you

detailSections.title: Coach Notes
detailSections.content:
The Swerve list is where the coaching value lives. Most clients know who their best customers are. Very few have explicitly decided who they will not work with. This action often produces an emotional response — "but I need the revenue." Help them calculate the true cost of bad-fit clients (including stress, time drain, and opportunity cost of not having space for great clients).

If a client cannot list 5 bad experiences, they either have not been in business long enough or they are not being honest. Push gently.

This pairs directly with the Purpose Statement — purpose defines the direction, Serve & Swerve defines the boundaries.

detailSections.title: What to Do
detailSections.content: Review your current business activities, client base, and daily work to identify where you are living your purpose and where you are drifting from it. Then create a plan to move towards alignment without destroying cash flow in the process.

detailSections.title: Why It Matters
detailSections.content: Most business owners have a gap between what they say matters and how they actually spend their time. That gap is where energy leaks, motivation dies, and burnout builds. This audit makes the gap visible — and once you can see it, you can close it. The goal is not overnight transformation. It is an honest look at where you are, so you can make deliberate choices about where you are heading.

detailSections.title: How to Use This
detailSections.content:
Work through each section with your Purpose Statement and Serve & Swerve criteria beside you. Score honestly — nobody sees this but you (and your coach, if you have one).

---

detailSections.title: Time Alignment
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Look at your calendar from last week** — Categorise every block of time: purpose-aligned work, necessary admin, reactive firefighting, or misaligned activity. What percentage of your time went to purpose-aligned work?
- [ ] ⚡ Quick Win · **Identify your biggest time thief** — The single activity that consumed the most time while contributing the least to your purpose. Can you eliminate, delegate, or reduce it this week?
- [ ] **Check your ratio** — If less than 30% of your time is purpose-aligned, something structural needs to change. If it is 30-60%, you are on track to improve. Above 60%, you are in good shape.

detailSections.title: Revenue Alignment
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **List your revenue sources** — Break down where your money comes from by client type, service type, or product type.
- [ ] **Mark each as Aligned, Neutral, or Misaligned** — Aligned means it directly serves your purpose and ideal clients. Misaligned means it pays bills but pulls you away from where you want to go. Neutral is somewhere in between.
- [ ] **Calculate the percentage** — What share of your revenue comes from aligned work? If misaligned revenue is over 40%, you have a strategic problem, not just a time management problem.

detailSections.title: Energy Alignment
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **List the 5 activities that give you energy at work** — The things that make you lose track of time, feel proud, or look forward to Monday. These are purpose clues.
- [ ] **List the 5 activities that drain your energy** — The things you procrastinate on, dread, or feel resentful about. These are misalignment signals.
- [ ] ⚡ Quick Win · **Can you delegate or eliminate one energy-draining activity this month?** — Not all of them. Just one. Start building momentum towards a more aligned workload.

detailSections.title: The Alignment Plan
detailSections.content:
- [ ] **Pick your biggest misalignment** — The one area (time, revenue, or energy) where the gap between purpose and reality is largest.
- [ ] **Set a 90-day target** — What would "better" look like in 90 days? Not perfect — just measurably better. Example: "Move from 20% aligned revenue to 40% by taking on 2 ideal clients and winding down 1 misaligned one."
- [ ] **Identify the one change that starts the shift** — Not a complete business overhaul. One deliberate change you can make this month that moves the needle.

---

detailSections.title: Expected Outcome
detailSections.content:
- A clear, honest picture of how aligned your business currently is with your purpose
- Specific areas where you are drifting and the cost of that drift
- A 90-day plan to increase alignment without blowing up your revenue
- Renewed clarity about what to pursue, what to tolerate temporarily, and what to actively eliminate

detailSections.title: Coach Notes
detailSections.content:
This action often reveals uncomfortable truths — clients discover that 60%+ of their revenue comes from work they do not enjoy or clients they do not want. The coaching moment is helping them create a transition plan rather than either ignoring the problem or making drastic changes that kill cash flow.

Revisit this audit every quarter. Alignment is not a one-time fix — it is an ongoing practice of choosing to close the gap between what you say matters and how you actually operate.

The sequence matters: Purpose Statement → Serve & Swerve → Alignment Audit. Do not run this audit without the first two, or they will not know what they are auditing against.
